 {"id":"wa-2mwk","title":"Circuit breaker infrastructure: state machine, cooldowns, and status reporting","description":"
# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CircuitBreaker struct with state machine
- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns
- [ ] Status visible in wa doctor
- [ ] Structured logging on state changes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-2ojrv","title":"Write integration tests for full asupersync stack","description":"# Write integration tests for full asupersync stack

## Goal
End-to-end tests exercising the complete FrankenTerm system on asupersync. These are the final validation before removing tokio.

## Test scenarios (each is a separate test function)

### 1. Full lifecycle (seed: 100)
Start runtime → create MuxPool → acquire connection → execute mux command → release → shutdown
Log: runtime init, pool create, acquire attempt/success, command send/recv, release, shutdown sequence

### 2. Concurrent pool operations (seed: 200)
Spawn 20 tasks, each acquiring from pool (max_size=5), executing command, releasing.
Log: per-task acquire/release, pool stats at each step, concurrency level over time

### 3. IPC round-trip (seed: 300)
Start IPC server → CLI client connects → sends JSON command → daemon processes → response returned → client disconnects
Log: server accept, request parse, command dispatch, response serialize, client disconnect

### 4. Event streaming (seed: 400)
Start native event listener → mock WezTerm sends events → verify channel receives all events in order
Log: event emit, channel reserve/commit, event receive, ordering verification

### 5. Mid-operation cancellation (seed: 500)
Start long-running operation (pane subscription) → cancel after 3 events → verify clean shutdown
Log: subscription start, each delta received, cancellation trigger, scope drain, obligation check

### 6. Timeout cascade (seed: 600)
Set tight Budget::deadline → operations that exceed deadline → verify proper Cancelled propagation through call stack
Log: budget set, operation start, deadline trigger, Cancelled propagation through each scope level

### 7. Connection recovery (seed: 700)
Acquire connection → simulate broken pipe → next acquire creates new connection → verify transparent recovery
Log: first acquire, pipe break injection, error detection, new connection create, recovery success

### 8. Full system chaos test (seed: 800, chaos enabled)
Full lifecycle with random failure injection: I/O errors, timeouts, channel closures.
Log: injection points, error handling paths taken, recovery actions, final state verification

## Logging format
All integration tests use structured JSON logging with:
```json
{
  "timestamp": "virtual_or_real",
  "test": "test_name",
  "seed": 123,
  "scope": "scope_id",
  "event": "acquire_start|acquire_ok|send_pdu|recv_pdu|...",
  "details": { ... }
}
```

## Acceptance criteria
- All 8 test scenarios pass
- Each test has detailed structured JSON logging
- Logs sufficient to diagnose any failure from log output alone
- No obligation leaks (oracle check in every test)
- No task leaks (oracle check in every test)
- Tests reproducible via seed
- Chaos test exercises at least 5 different failure injection points

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Integration test determinism with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) for the concurrent test scenarios (scenarios 2, 4, 5, 7) to ensure correctness under all relevant interleavings. For each DPOR-explored scenario:
  - Set max_schedules based on task count (e.g., 100 schedules for 5-task scenarios, 1000 for 20-task)
  - Verify all oracle checks pass under every explored schedule
  - Log the total number of schedules explored and any schedule that triggered a new code path

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for stack throughput**: Add `benches/integration_throughput.rs` measuring:
  - Full lifecycle throughput: complete cycles/sec (create pool → acquire → command → release)
  - Concurrent pool throughput: aggregate ops/sec with N=5,10,20 concurrent tasks
  - IPC round-trip latency under load (1, 10, 100 concurrent clients)
  - Event streaming throughput: events/sec through the full pipeline (emit → channel → consume)
  - End-to-end latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) for representative workloads","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:12.522797Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:52:02.707091Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2ojrv","depends_on_id":"wa-22x4r","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:52:03.718609Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2ojrv","depends_on_id":"wa-181uk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:52:03.911647Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2oph2","title":"SIMD-accelerated pane output processing — vectorized scanning and compression","description":"# SIMD-Accelerated Output Processing

## Skills: /alien-artifact-coding, /extreme-software-optimization

## Problem
200 panes producing output simultaneously. Each pane output must be scanned for patterns, compressed for storage, and forwarded to observers. At scale, this is CPU-bound.

## Solution: Vectorized processing pipeline

### 1. SIMD pattern scanning
Use SIMD (AVX2/NEON) to scan pane output for:
- Newline boundaries (for line counting)
- ANSI escape sequence detection (for stripping)
- Pattern triggers (e.g., error keywords, completion markers)
- UTF-8 validation

Reference: /alien-artifact-coding for formal correctness proof of SIMD scanning.

### 2. Vectorized compression
- Use zstd with SIMD acceleration for scrollback compression
- Batch multiple pane outputs into single compression calls
- Pre-trained dictionary for terminal output (high compression ratio)
- **Plugs into wa-2ahu0 memory engine**: provides the compression primitives that the memory pressure engine calls for scrollback compression/eviction

### 3. Zero-copy output pipeline
- mmap-based buffer for pane output capture
- Process in-place without allocation
- Only copy when observer needs a snapshot

### 4. Benchmarks (/extreme-software-optimization)
Must profile and prove:
- Pattern scan throughput: >2GB/s per core
- Compression throughput: >500MB/s per core
- Zero-copy reduces allocation by >90% vs current

## Tests
- **Criterion benchmarks** for pattern scanning (varying output patterns: plain text, ANSI-heavy, binary-heavy)
- **Criterion benchmarks** for compression (terminal output corpus with pre-trained dictionary)
- Throughput test: 200 panes producing 10MB/s each, verify no backpressure
- SIMD correctness: identical results to scalar fallback on ALL inputs (property-based testing with proptest)
- **Platform tests**: AVX2 on x86_64, NEON on aarch64 (Apple Silicon), scalar fallback everywhere

## Acceptance criteria
- SIMD scanning >2GB/s throughput
- Compression >500MB/s throughput with >5:1 ratio on terminal output
- Zero-copy pipeline reduces allocations measurably
- Scalar fallback for non-SIMD platforms
- Formally verified correctness (/alien-artifact-coding)

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks**: Must demonstrate >4x improvement over scalar baseline for all SIMD paths:
  - Newline scanning: benchmark SIMD vs scalar on 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB buffers. SIMD must achieve >4x speedup.
  - ANSI escape detection: benchmark on ANSI-heavy terminal output (50%+ escape sequences). SIMD must achieve >4x speedup.
  - UTF-8 validation: benchmark against std::str::from_utf8 baseline. SIMD must achieve >4x speedup on valid UTF-8 and equivalent error detection on invalid.
  - Compression: benchmark zstd with and without SIMD. Measure both throughput and compression ratio.
- **Proptest for SIMD/scalar equivalence**: For every SIMD-accelerated function, generate random byte sequences and verify the SIMD result is bit-identical to the scalar fallback:
  - Newline positions must match exactly
  - ANSI escape boundaries must match exactly
  - UTF-8 validation accept/reject decisions must match exactly
  - Compression/decompression roundtrip must produce identical output

## macOS Compatibility (Apple Silicon)
- **NEON on Apple Silicon**: All Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3/M4) support NEON SIMD. Use `std::arch::aarch64` intrinsics with `#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]`.
- **Test both AVX2 and NEON paths**: CI must test AVX2 on x86_64 Linux/macOS and NEON on aarch64 macOS. Use runtime feature detection (`is_x86_feature_detected!("avx2")`) on x86_64 and compile-time `#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]` for NEON (always available on Apple Silicon).
- **Scalar fallback**: Always available for platforms with neither AVX2 nor NEON. The scalar path is the reference implementation for correctness testing.
- **Benchmark all three paths**: Criterion benchmarks should include NEON results on Apple Silicon alongside AVX2 results on x86_64, with scalar as the baseline for both.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3bja.3** (Ghostty I/O pipeline): Ghostty achieves extreme I/O performance using SIMD-accelerated terminal parsing in Zig. This bead applies the same philosophy in Rust — study Ghostty's approach to vectorized escape sequence parsing and adapt the techniques. The two beads share performance targets and may share benchmark infrastructure.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:12:09.705391Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:47:03.879984Z"}

 {"id":"wa-2pir","title":"Web server tests: /health + basic endpoint contract (feature web)","description":"# Task: Web server tests (feature web)

## Goal
Prevent the optional web server from silently rotting.

The web server is not on the critical path, but when enabled it must be reliable, safe, and schema-stable.

## Test scope
- Runs under `cargo test --features web`.
- Fully offline (no real WezTerm required).

## Required test cases
- Lifecycle:
  - start server on `--port 0`
  - hit `/health`
  - shut down cleanly

- Contract:
  - `/health` schema is parseable and stable
  - `/panes`, `/events`, `/search` responses are:
    - valid JSON
    - stable ordering where applicable
    - redacted (no raw secrets)

- Safety:
  - default bind is localhost
  - public bind requires explicit `--dangerous-bind-any`

## Observability in tests
- Capture request logs/spans and assert a few key fields exist (method/path/status/latency).
- Assert that sensitive values do not appear in logs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo test --features web` includes at least:
  - `/health` responds
  - endpoint response bodies are schema-parseable
  - redaction + bind-default invariants are enforced


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative test that attempts `--dangerous-bind-any` without explicit confirmation (if applicable) and ensure it is rejected.
  - Add an assertion that there are no write/mutation endpoints registered.

 {"id":"wa-809y","title":"Workflow audit integration: record per-step actions/outcomes into audit trail","description":"# Task: Workflow audit integration

## Goal
Ensure workflows are explainable and trustworthy by recording an audit trail for every workflow action attempt.

Workflows can:
- send text
- issue control sequences (Ctrl-C)
- wait for conditions
- perform account/device-auth steps (later)

Users need to know exactly what wa did and why.

## Requirements
- For each workflow execution:
  - record an audit entry for each step action attempt (even if denied or failed)
  - include `step_id` / `step_name` / `workflow_name`
  - include policy decision and preconditions observed
  - include verification outcomes (wait-for matched, timeout)

- Redaction:
  - never store raw secrets or full inputs
  - store hashes/summaries consistent with the redaction system

- Correlation:
  - link audit entries to `workflow_execution_id`
  - link to the originating `event_id` when available

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - run a synthetic workflow that attempts a send and a wait
  - verify audit timeline entries exist for both attempts and correlate to the workflow execution
- Redaction tests:
  - ensure the audit record never contains raw inputs/pane tails

## Acceptance Criteria
- A workflow run produces a coherent audit timeline that matches the workflow_step_log.
- Failures still produce audit entries that explain what was attempted and what was denied.

 {"id":"wa-8bk","title":"[EPIC] UX Excellence: Explainability, Ergonomics, and User Delight","description":"# [EPIC] UX Excellence: Explainability, Ergonomics, and User Delight

## Mission
Transform wa from a capable but opaque automation tool into a **transparent, delightful, and trustworthy** system that users love to use.

## Why This Matters
Terminal automation is inherently scary. Users cannot see what wa is doing, and when things go wrong, they often do not understand why. This creates:
- **Fear**: "What if it sends to the wrong pane?"
- **Frustration**: "Why did it deny my send?"
- **Distrust**: "I do not know what it is doing"

Great UX transforms these into:
- **Confidence**: "I can preview before executing"
- **Understanding**: "I know exactly why it made that decision"
- **Trust**: "I can see everything happening in real-time"

## Core Insight: Legibility
The unifying theme across all these improvements is **legibility**—making wa's behavior visible and understandable:
1. **Legible decisions** → Deep Explainability (`wa why` + enriched errors)
2. **Legible state** → Interactive TUI Dashboard
3. **Legible intentions** → Dry-run preview mode
4. **Legible history** → Event timeline with correlation
5. **Legible next steps** → Quick-fix suggestions

## Scope (30 Improvements)
This epic encompasses improvements across multiple dimensions:

### Reliability & Robustness (5)
- Watchdog/heartbeat with auto-restart
- Circuit breaker for failed services
- Chaos testing harness
- Retry with exponential backoff
- Graceful degradation modes

### Performance (5)
- Lazy pattern compilation
- Bloom filter pre-filter
- Connection pooling for WezTerm CLI
- Background compression
- Incremental FTS sync

### User Experience & Ergonomics (5)
- Interactive TUI dashboard (expand existing)
- Shell completions for all commands
- Progressive disclosure in output
- Smart aliases
- Natural language event descriptions

### Intuitiveness & Discoverability (5)
- `wa why` command for decision explanation
- `wa learn` interactive tutorial
- Contextual help in every error
- Smart defaults based on environment detection
- In-CLI documentation

### Usefulness & Compelling Features (5)
- Session recording and replay
- Event timeline with cross-pane correlation
- Notification webhooks (Slack/Discord/email)
- Export to markdown/HTML
- Proactive suggestions ("Account X at 85%")

### Safety & Trust (5)
- Dry-run for all human commands
- Confirmation prompts with policy reason
- Rollback log visualization
- Simulation mode (mock WezTerm)
- Plugin/extension system for custom patterns

## Success Criteria
- New users can understand wa's behavior within 10 minutes
- Errors are self-explanatory and include actionable fixes
- Users can preview any action before executing
- The TUI provides real-time visibility into all operations
- Support burden decreases because users can self-serve

## Testing
Each sub-feature has its own testing requirements. At the epic level:
- User testing with 3+ external developers (collect feedback)
- Measure time-to-first-success for new users
- Track error resolution time (should decrease)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 30 improvement areas have implemented beads with tests
- Documentation covers all new commands and features
- TUI is polished and accessible (keyboard navigation, screen reader support)
- Error messages meet the "actionable fix included" standard

 {"id":"wa-d248l","title":"Phase 3c: Rename feature flags vendored→frankenterm","description":"Rename feature flags in wa-core and wa Cargo.toml files:

## wa-core/Cargo.toml changes
- New: frankenterm = ["frankenterm-deps"]
- New: frankenterm-deps = ["dep:codec", "dep:config", "dep:mux", "dep:wezterm-term"]
- Changed: vendored = ["frankenterm"] (backward compat alias)
- Changed: vendored-wezterm = ["frankenterm-deps"] (backward compat alias)

## wa/Cargo.toml changes
- New: frankenterm = ["wa-core/frankenterm"]
- Changed: vendored = ["frankenterm"] (backward compat alias)

## Rationale
The name "vendored" no longer applies — we own the code. "frankenterm" signals the project's philosophy of combining best pieces from multiple sources. Backward compat aliases ensure existing CI/scripts/muscle-memory continue to work.

 {"id":"wa-jl5","title":"E2E script: notification webhook delivery (mock server + retry + throttle)","description":"# E2E script: notification webhook delivery (mock server + retry + throttle)

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that webhook notifications:
- are emitted for matching events
- are delivered to configured endpoints
- retry with backoff on failure
- respect throttling limits
- recover cleanly from temporary network/server failure

This validates the notification system (`wa-psm`) in real wiring, under the shared E2E contract.

## Key constraints
- Deterministic synchronization: **no fixed `sleep N`** waits.
- Safe-by-default artifacts: payloads must be redacted and must not include transcript contents.
- Uses the standard harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).

## Test setup
- Start a local mock webhook server (owned by the E2E harness) with:
  - configurable response sequence (e.g., 500, 500, 200)
  - a `/received` endpoint that returns received payloads as JSON
  - a `/attempt_count` endpoint
  - request logging to an artifacts file

- Start `wa watch` in a dedicated E2E workspace with:
  - notifications configured to point at the mock server
  - a deterministic event source (dummy panes) that can emit known patterns

## Scenarios

### 1) Successful delivery
```bash
# Configure mock to return 200 OK
mock_server --responses 200 &

# Trigger an event that should send notification (via dummy pane output)
wa e2e emit --pane 3 --event codex.usage_limit_reached

# Wait until mock server has >=1 received payload (poll with timeout)
mock_server wait --received-min 1 --timeout 10s

# Assert payload shape + key fields
mock_server dump --received > artifacts/notifications_received.json
jq -e '.[-1].event_type == "codex.usage_limit_reached"' artifacts/notifications_received.json
```

### 2) Retry on failure (backoff)
```bash
# Fail first 2, then succeed
mock_server --responses 500,500,200 &

wa e2e emit --pane 3 --event session.compaction

# Wait until attempt_count >= 3 (poll with timeout)
mock_server wait --attempts-min 3 --timeout 30s

# Assert delivered exactly once (or at least once, depending on idempotency rules)
mock_server dump --received > artifacts/notifications_received.json
jq -e 'map(select(.event_type=="session.compaction")) | length >= 1' artifacts/notifications_received.json
```

### 3) Throttling
```bash
# Configure low throttle limit via an E2E config file for the workspace
# (avoid mutating global user config)

wa e2e emit --pane 3 --event session.compaction --repeat 5 --burst

# Wait for ingestion + notification processing to quiesce (bounded)
wa e2e wait-quiescent --timeout 20s

# Assert at most N delivered in the throttle window
mock_server dump --received > artifacts/notifications_received.json
jq -e 'length <= 2' artifacts/notifications_received.json
```

### 4) Network failure recovery
```bash
# Start with server down
wa e2e emit --pane 3 --event session.compaction

# Bring server up
mock_server --responses 200 &

# Wait for delivery
mock_server wait --received-min 1 --timeout 30s
```

## Artifacts
- `mock_server.log` (request log + timestamps)
- `notifications_received.json` (redacted payloads)
- `wa_watch.log` (watcher logs)
- `events.jsonl` (event evidence)
- `policy_audit_slice.jsonl` (proof we did not leak secrets)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging contract
- All steps log:
  - test case name
  - watcher/workspace identifiers
  - mock server bind addr
  - timeouts + outcomes

Example:
```
[NOTIFY_E2E] mock_server started addr=127.0.0.1:8080 responses=500,500,200
[NOTIFY_E2E] emit event=session.compaction pane=3
[NOTIFY_E2E] wait attempts>=3 timeout=30s
[NOTIFY_E2E] delivered event=session.compaction attempts=3
```

## Testing
- E2E determinism requirements:
  - no fixed sleeps
  - explicit timeouts on every wait
  - fail with an actionable summary and artifact pointers

- Payload safety requirements:
  - assert payloads contain no raw pane transcripts
  - assert payloads contain no secret-like strings (run the standard redaction scan used by `wa-4vx.10.18`)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Notifications are delivered to the mock webhook for a known event.
- [ ] Retry/backoff behavior is observable (attempt_count increases, eventual delivery).
- [ ] Throttling limits deliveries deterministically.
- [ ] Temporary server unavailability recovers and delivers queued notifications.
- [ ] Artifacts are produced and contain enough context to debug failures.

 {"id":"wa-o8j","title":"WAL recovery: automatic checkpoint and journal cleanup on startup","description":"# Task: WAL Recovery on Startup

## Goal
Handle unclean shutdown scenarios gracefully by recovering WAL/journal state.

## Problem
If wa crashes or is killed while writing:
- WAL file may be large (uncommitted transactions)
- Journal file may exist (transaction in progress)
- Next startup should recover cleanly

## Implementation

### Startup Recovery Sequence
```rust
impl StorageHandle {
    pub fn open_with_recovery(path: &Path) -> Result<Self> {
        // 1. Check for recovery situation
        let wal_path = path.with_extension("sqlite-wal");
        let journal_path = path.with_extension("sqlite-journal");
        
        if wal_path.exists() || journal_path.exists() {
            tracing::info\!(
                wal_exists = wal_path.exists(),
                journal_exists = journal_path.exists(),
                "Recovery situation detected, will attempt recovery"
            );
        }
        
        // 2. Open with recovery pragmas
        let conn = Connection::open(path)?;
        
        // 3. Run quick integrity check
        let result: String = conn.query_row(
            "PRAGMA quick_check",
            [],
            |row| row.get(0)
        )?;
        
        if result \!= "ok" {
            tracing::error\!(result = %result, "Database corruption detected");
            return Err(StorageError::Corruption { details: result });
        }
        
        // 4. Checkpoint WAL if large
        let (busy, log, ckpt): (i32, i32, i32) = conn.query_row(
            "PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(PASSIVE)",
            [],
            |row| Ok((row.get(0)?, row.get(1)?, row.get(2)?))
        )?;
        
        tracing::info\!(
            busy = busy,
            wal_frames = log,
            checkpointed = ckpt,
            "WAL checkpoint completed"
        );
        
        // 5. If WAL is huge, do full checkpoint
        if log > 10000 {
            tracing::warn\!(frames = log, "Large WAL detected, doing full checkpoint");
            conn.execute("PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)", [])?;
        }
        
        Ok(Self { conn })
    }
}
```

### Logging Contract
On recovery:
```
INFO  wa::storage: Recovery situation detected wal_exists=true journal_exists=false
INFO  wa::storage: Quick integrity check passed
INFO  wa::storage: WAL checkpoint completed busy=0 wal_frames=5432 checkpointed=5432
INFO  wa::storage: Database recovery complete
```

On corruption:
```
ERROR wa::storage: Database corruption detected result="malformed disk image"
ERROR wa::storage: Cannot recover automatically, run: wa db repair
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: mock WAL/journal scenarios
- Integration: kill wa mid-write, verify recovery on restart
- E2E: wa-4vx.10.12 (workflow resume after restart) covers this path

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unclean shutdown followed by restart recovers automatically
- Large WAL files are checkpointed on startup
- Corruption detected and surfaced clearly
- Recovery actions logged for debugging

## Vision
Build a **flight recorder** for FrankenTerm that captures **all text entering and leaving the mux server** in an ultra-performant append-only pipeline, then exposes fast, trustworthy, hybrid lexical/semantic retrieval for operators and agents.

This epic is intentionally broader than simple logging. It defines a full system:
1. Capture/tap points for ingress and egress text
2. Durable append-only write path under high concurrency
3. Indexing and retrieval architecture (lexical + semantic)
4. Query/API surfaces for humans and robots
5. Reliability, observability, and rollout safety

## Why this matters
- Multi-agent terminal swarms generate high-volume, high-value forensic data.
- Current observability is good for event/state workflows, but not yet a true end-to-end immutable transcript plane optimized for forensic replay and hybrid retrieval.
- A robust flight recorder increases debugging speed, incident response quality, postmortem accuracy, and model/operator feedback loops.

## Design intent
- **Performance first**: append-only, batched writes, minimal per-event overhead.
- **Correctness first**: total ordering/causality metadata sufficient for replay and audit.
- **Durability first**: resilient write path (including exploration of FrankenSQLite multi-writer and WAL self-healing capabilities).
- **Search first**: Tantivy-backed lexical retrieval plus semantic retrieval and rank fusion.
- **Operator first**: stable CLI/robot/MCP interfaces and runbooks.

## Research grounding
Implementation must be informed by close study/adaptation patterns from:
- `/dp/frankensqlite`
- `/dp/coding_agent_session_search` (cass)
- `/dp/xf`

## Non-goals (for this epic)
- Replacing all existing storage paths in one unsafe big-bang migration.
- Shipping semantic retrieval without lexical fallback and measurable quality baselines.
- Adding unbounded retention without explicit cost/retention controls.

## Deliverables
- Full bead graph (research -> architecture -> implementation -> validation -> rollout)
- Feature-gated implementation plan with rollback path
- Search quality/perf test strategy and success thresholds
- Operator docs and migration guidance

## Success criteria
- Flight recorder captures ingress+egress text with stable ordering metadata.
- Append-only writer sustains swarm-scale load without destabilizing watcher/runtime.
- Lexical and hybrid search return correct results with measurable latency/quality targets.
- End-to-end system is testable, observable, and safe to roll out incrementally.

## Acceptance criteria
- All child tracks are completed or explicitly deferred with rationale.
- Dependency graph is acyclic and implementation-ready for swarm execution.

Comprehensive unit test suite for every session persistence component, designed to run WITHOUT a real WezTerm mux server, using mocks and fixtures for fast CI execution and thorough coverage.

## Background & Motivation
The session persistence system (wa-rsaf epic) has 12 beads spanning schema, storage, capture, orchestration, restoration, and CLI. Each component needs isolated unit tests that verify correctness independently, long before the full E2E roundtrip can be tested. Unit tests catch bugs that E2E tests miss: serialization edge cases, SQL migration correctness, tree reconstruction corner cases, content-hash collisions, and encoding issues. They also serve as living documentation for the data formats and algorithms.

 {"id":"wa-rsaf.2","title":"Formal session restoration verification via proptest","description":"## Goal
Use property-based testing (proptest) to formally verify session restoration correctness: for all valid mux states S, restore(snapshot(S)) is approximately equal to S (isomorphic up to PIDs and timestamps).

## Background and Motivation
Session persistence (wa-rsaf) is the most critical feature -- if restore produces incorrect state, users lose work. Traditional unit tests check specific cases but cannot cover the full state space. Property-based testing generates thousands of random valid states and verifies the isomorphism property holds for ALL of them.

## Technical Design

### Isomorphism Specification
Define approximately equal for mux states:
```rust
fn is_isomorphic(original: &MuxSnapshot, restored: &MuxSnapshot) -> bool {
    // Layout topology must be identical
    original.layout_tree.is_structurally_equal(&restored.layout_tree)
    // Pane count must match
    && original.panes.len() == restored.panes.len()
    // Scrollback content must match (up to trailing whitespace)
    && original.panes.iter().zip(restored.panes.iter())
        .all(|(a, b)| a.scrollback_trimmed() == b.scrollback_trimmed())
    // Cursor positions must match
    && original.panes.iter().zip(restored.panes.iter())
        .all(|(a, b)| a.cursor == b.cursor)
    // Working directories must match
    && original.panes.iter().zip(restored.panes.iter())
        .all(|(a, b)| a.cwd == b.cwd)
    // PIDs may differ (new processes)
    // Timestamps may differ (restored at different time)
}
```

### Property-Based Strategy
```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn snapshot_restore_roundtrip(state in arb_mux_state()) {
        let snapshot = capture_snapshot(&state)?;
        let restored = restore_snapshot(&snapshot)?;
        prop_assert!(is_isomorphic(&state, &restored));
    }
}
```

### Arbitrary State Generation
Generate valid mux states with configurable complexity:
- 1-20 windows, 1-10 tabs per window, 1-5 panes per tab
- Random split directions (horizontal/vertical) with valid ratios
- Scrollback: 0-10000 lines of random text with ANSI escapes
- Cursor positions within valid bounds
- Working directories from a pool of valid paths
- Agent types from valid set

### Edge Case Properties
```rust
// Empty state roundtrips
prop_assert!(is_isomorphic(&empty_state(), &restore(snapshot(empty_state()))));

// Single pane roundtrips
proptest!(|s in arb_single_pane_state()| ...);

// Maximum complexity roundtrips
proptest!(|s in arb_max_complexity_state()| ...);

// Mid-write snapshot (concurrent modification)
proptest!(|s in arb_state_with_pending_writes()| ...);

// Partial failure recovery
proptest!(|s in arb_state(), fail_point in arb_fail_point()| {
    // Inject failure during restore, verify partial state is consistent
});
```

### Implementation Location
- New: crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_snapshot.rs
- Depends on: wa-rsaf snapshot infrastructure

## Existing Code References
- wa-rsaf beads: MuxSnapshot schema, SnapshotEngine, layout restoration, scrollback injection
- proptest crate: strategy generation framework

## Dependencies
- Depends on wa-rsaf session persistence (needs snapshot/restore to be implemented first)
- Should be created alongside or immediately after wa-rsaf implementation

## Acceptance Criteria
- proptest strategies for arbitrary valid mux states
- Roundtrip property: restore(snapshot(S)) approximately equals S verified for 10000+ random states
- Edge cases: empty, single-pane, max-complexity, mid-write, partial-failure
- Isomorphism specification documented and implemented
- Regression: any snapshot/restore change must pass all proptest cases
- Run time: < 60 seconds for full proptest suite

## Specific Proptest Strategies

### Arbitrary Layout Trees
```rust
/// Generate arbitrary valid layout trees with controlled complexity.
fn arb_layout_tree() -> impl Strategy<Value = PaneNode> {
    let leaf = (arb_pane_id(), arb_cwd(), arb_pane_size())
        .prop_map(|(id, cwd, size)| PaneNode::Leaf {
            pane_id: id, cwd, size,
        });

    // Recursive: split nodes with 2 children, up to 6 levels deep
    leaf.prop_recursive(
        6,    // max depth
        50,   // max nodes
        2,    // items per collection (binary splits)
        |inner| {
            (arb_split_direction(), inner.clone(), inner, arb_ratio())
                .prop_map(|(dir, left, right, ratio)| PaneNode::Split {
                    direction: dir,
                    children: vec![left, right],
                    ratio,
                })
        },
    )
}

fn arb_pane_size() -> impl Strategy<Value = PaneSize> {
    (1u16..=500, 1u16..=200)
        .prop_map(|(cols, rows)| PaneSize { cols, rows })
}

fn arb_split_direction() -> impl Strategy<Value = SplitDirection> {
    prop_oneof![
        Just(SplitDirection::Horizontal),
        Just(SplitDirection::Vertical),
    ]
}

fn arb_ratio() -> impl Strategy<Value = f64> {
    (10u32..=90).prop_map(|r| r as f64 / 100.0)
}
```

### Random Scrollback Content
```rust
/// Generate random scrollback with realistic terminal output characteristics.
fn arb_scrollback_content() -> impl Strategy<Value = Vec<ScrollbackLine>> {
    prop::collection::vec(arb_scrollback_line(), 0..10000)
}

fn arb_scrollback_line() -> impl Strategy<Value = ScrollbackLine> {
    prop_oneof![
        // Plain text lines (most common)
        60 => "[a-zA-Z0-9 .,:;!?-]{0,200}"
            .prop_map(|text| ScrollbackLine::plain(text)),
        // Lines with ANSI color attributes
        20 => (arb_sgr_params(), ".{0,150}")
            .prop_map(|(sgr, text)| ScrollbackLine::attributed(text, sgr)),
        // Lines with wide characters (CJK)
        10 => prop::collection::vec(arb_cjk_char(), 0..80)
            .prop_map(|chars| ScrollbackLine::plain(
                chars.into_iter().collect::<String>()
            )),
        // Lines with hyperlinks (OSC 8)
        5 => (arb_url(), ".{1,50}")
            .prop_map(|(url, label)| ScrollbackLine::hyperlink(url, label)),
        // Empty lines
        5 => Just(ScrollbackLine::plain(String::new())),
    ]
}

fn arb_sgr_params() -> impl Strategy<Value = Vec<SgrParam>> {
    prop::collection::vec(
        prop_oneof![
            Just(SgrParam::Bold),
            Just(SgrParam::Italic),
            Just(SgrParam::Underline),
            (0u8..=255).prop_map(SgrParam::FgColor256),
            (0u8..=255).prop_map(SgrParam::BgColor256),
            (0u8..=255, 0u8..=255, 0u8..=255)
                .prop_map(|(r, g, b)| SgrParam::FgColorRgb(r, g, b)),
        ],
        0..5,
    )
}
```

/// Generate random process configurations for snapshot restoration testing.
fn arb_process_config() -> impl Strategy<Value = ProcessInfo> {
    prop_oneof![

            .prop_map(|(shell, cwd, env)| ProcessInfo {

            .prop_map(|(cmd, cwd, env)| ProcessInfo {
                command: vec![cmd],
                cwd, env,
                process_type: ProcessType::Unknown,

}

 {"id":"wa-ruus","title":"Track 1: Basics - What is wa? Start watching. View status (5 min)","description":"# Track 1: Basics

## Purpose
Introduce wa to new users in under 5 minutes. They should understand what wa does, successfully start it, and see meaningful output.

## Target Audience
Users who just installed wa and want to understand what it does.

## Exercise Sequence

### Exercise 1.1: What is wa?
- **Type**: Information
- **Content**: Brief explanation with ASCII diagram
- **Duration**: 30 seconds
- **Goal**: User understands wa is a "terminal hypervisor for AI agents"

### Exercise 1.2: Check WezTerm
- **Type**: Verification
- **Requires**: WezTerm running
- **Action**: User runs `wa doctor` (or tutorial auto-checks)
- **Success**: "✓ WezTerm detected with 2 panes"
- **Fallback**: Simulation mode shows mock output

### Exercise 1.3: Start Watching
- **Type**: Interactive
- **Requires**: WezTerm running
- **Action**: User runs `wa watch` (tutorial may auto-start)
- **Success**: "wa is now watching 2 panes"
- **Duration**: 60 seconds

### Exercise 1.4: View Status
- **Type**: Interactive
- **Action**: User runs `wa status`
- **Success**: Tutorial explains each column
- **Duration**: 60 seconds

### Exercise 1.5: Your First Event
- **Type**: Simulated (unless agent present)
- **Action**: Tutorial injects mock compaction event
- **Success**: User runs `wa events` and sees it
- **Duration**: 90 seconds

### Track Completion
- Achievement unlocked: "First Watch"
- Prompt to continue to Track 2

## Testing
- Golden output tests for each exercise explanation
- Integration test for full track in sandbox mode
- E2E test with real WezTerm (optional)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5 exercises implemented
- [ ] Each exercise has clear instructions and success criteria
- [ ] Sandbox mode works for all exercises
- [ ] Track completion triggers achievement
- [ ] Total time <5 minutes in testing","notes":"Implemented Basics track as 5 exercises in crates/wa-core/src/learn.rs (what-is-wa, doctor check, start watcher, view status, first event). Enabled simulation mode for all basics exercises. Updated achievement mapping so first_watch unlocks on basics.3 and first_event on basics.5/events.1. Added regression assertions for 5 exercises + all-simulatable basics.  {"id":"wa-upg.9.6","title":"E2E: triage with multiple simultaneous issues (artifacts + suggested actions)","description":"# Task: E2E triage multi-issue scenario

Validate that triage prioritization works in realistic multi-symptom situations.

Create multiple issues:
- a recent crash bundle
- a repeated event with escalation
- a stuck workflow
- a health warning (index lag)

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps; use wait-for/quiescence.
- Artifacts include:
  - triage JSON output
  - logs for each simulated issue

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E demonstrates operator usefulness (clear ordering and actions).

 {"id":"wa-uyve","title":"[EPIC] Saved searches + scheduled alerts","description":"## Background
Operators repeatedly run the same search/event filters to track specific agent states. Today this is manual and easy to forget.

## Goals
- Persist named searches (query + filters + scope) for reuse
- Allow scheduled runs that emit notifications when results appear
- Integrate with CLI/TUI/web dashboards without duplicating search logic

## Non-Goals
- New query language (reuse existing FTS syntax)
- External alert delivery (handled in notification channels epic)

## Considerations
- Saved search must be safe to execute (no side effects)
- Scheduling should be rate-limited and observable
- Outputs must be redacted per safety config

## Success Criteria
- Users can create/list/run/delete saved searches
- Scheduled alerts fire with clear context and can be disabled
- Full unit + e2e test coverage with verbose logs","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:01:03.835297223Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.253264-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T20:46:29.507396585Z","close_reason":"All child beads completed; closing stale-open epic to keep active plan set non-duplicative."}
